chain_of_thought: Top native frame before crash is strlen_default, but this is a libc helper; the first app-native frame is Java_com_dreamsecurity_dstoolkit_util_Base64__1decode in libDSToolkitV30Jni.so.

Termination in strlen usually indicates it was given a non-null-terminated or invalid pointer; this points to misuse of C strings inside the JNI decode implementation, likely taking a char* from Java String without validating embedded nulls or lengths.

MCP access to the exact native implementation failed (404 when querying Ghidra for the symbol), so I cannot see the concrete memcpy/strlen call sites or pointer arithmetic, only infer from the stack and Java-side signature.

On the Java side, Base64._decode(String) is a private native method used by public decode(String). Java validates only non-null/non-empty; it does not constrain length or contents of the String, so arbitrary attacker-controlled string data can reach the JNI function unchanged.

Because crash is in strlen_default, not in allocator/sanitizer, this is a direct library call failing on the provided buffer; typical patterns: using GetStringUTFChars and then calling strlen on data that may not be valid modified UTF-8, or mismanaging lifetime / secondary buffers, but all of these are app/native logic bugs, not harness-only issues.

The fuzz harness calls fuzz_one_input → Java_com_dreamsecurity_dstoolkit_util_Base64__1decode directly; this matches realistic app usage, because the normal app also passes arbitrary data from callers into Base64.decode, so this is a reachable path, not an artificial JNI signature mismatch or NULL-by-harness only.

Even though we lack the decompiled C, the combination of (1) crash in strlen, (2) app-native JNI frame just below, (3) fully attacker-controlled String length/content, and (4) no evidence of prior validation strongly suggests a genuine memory safety bug: out-of-bounds read, or dereference of invalid pointer from miscomputed buffer before strlen.

However, without native code view, I cannot state exact CWE type (likely buffer over-read or null deref), nor whether this can escalate beyond DoS; but at minimum, it is a reliable native crash on malformed input, reachable from Java, thus a meaningful DoS.

Given these uncertainties, I treat this as a likely but not fully proven vulnerability, with medium severity for now and theoretical exploitability; exploitation beyond DoS (e.g., RCE) cannot be argued convincingly with the current data.
reasons: Crash occurs in strlen_default called from app JNI function Java_com_dreamsecurity_dstoolkit_util_Base64__1decode, indicating misuse of C strings in native code rather than a harness-only artifact.

Java-side Base64.decode(String) passes attacker-controlled String data directly into the native _decode(String) without bounds checks apart from non-empty, so arbitrary content/length reaches the vulnerable native code.
Termination in strlen implies invalid pointer or missing terminator, typically from out-of-bounds access or mishandled buffers/pointers, which is classic memory-safety behaviour.
The same JNI entry point is used in production (not a dedicated fuzz-only method), so crashes here represent at least a realistic native DoS and potentially more severe memory corruption.
recommendations: Manually inspect and decompile Java_com_dreamsecurity_dstoolkit_util_Base64__1decode in libDSToolkitV30Jni.so to locate strlen and any related buffer handling; ensure all strings/buffers are length-bounded.
Replace raw strlen / C-string handling with length-aware APIs (e.g., track Java String length explicitly via GetStringUTFRegion / GetStringLength and avoid assuming null-termination).
Add robust input validation and defensive checks in the JNI decode implementation and, if possible, add fuzz tests around Base64.decode to catch regressions.
assumptions: The crash stack ordering is accurate and strlen_default is called directly or indirectly by Java_com_dreamsecurity_dstoolkit_util_Base64__1decode.
The fuzz harness does not violate the JNI contract beyond what normal Java callers can do (i.e., it uses a regular String argument).
The same native function and code path are used in production builds and not replaced by stubs or alternate implementations at runtime.
limitations: Failed to retrieve decompiled native implementation of Java_com_dreamsecurity_dstoolkit_util_Base64__1decode due to tool/server errors, so exact instruction-level behaviour is unknown.
Cannot conclusively determine if the bug is out-of-bounds read, null pointer dereference, or other memory misuse without native disassembly.
No visibility into app-level data sources feeding Base64.decode, so exact attacker control channels (network, IPC, file) are inferred but not confirmed.

exploit.trigger_method: Attacker-controlled String passed to com.dreamsecurity.dstoolkit.util.Base64.decode(String), reaching JNI _decode and crashing in strlen_default.
prerequisites: Attacker can cause the app to invoke Base64.decode(String) with chosen or influenced data (e.g., via network payload, file, or IPC).
App is using the vulnerable libDSToolkitV30Jni.so build on the device.
exploit_pipeline: Craft an input string containing malformed or extremely long Base64-like data that exercises edge cases in the native decode routine.
Deliver this string through the app feature that eventually calls Base64.decode(String) (e.g., a QR code, server response, or user-entered field).
When the app processes this string, it calls the JNI _decode, which in turn calls strlen_default on an invalid buffer, causing a native crash (denial of service).
poc_commands: adb shell am start -n <package>/<MainActivity> --es payload "AAAA...."  # payload must be routed to Base64.decode(String) in the app
adb logcat | grep -i "libDSToolkitV30Jni"  # observe native crash in strlen_default
exploit.notes: Without the native implementation, exploitation beyond a repeatable DoS (e.g., achieving control over instruction pointer) cannot be demonstrated, but the crash in strlen from attacker-controlled input indicates at least a native-level DoS vulnerability.
